Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods

Title Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods
Citation
Credibility C. Priyant Mark is an academic researcher from Manipal Institute of Technology. Aerospace engineer specializing in space debris removal, propulsion systems & CFD simulations. On a mission to protect Earth’s orbit through sustainable innovation.
Summary This paper conducts a comprehensive literature review of the then-current space debris removal methods, delving into many papers that propose different methods and highlighting the pros and cons of each method. Mission feasibility was prioritized, and the authors did not consider prediction models as sufficient evidence and proof of a method's effectiveness, prioritizing methods that had real missions launched for them. As a result, tether-based methods and dynamical systems-based methods were considered the most feasible.
Relevance Further supports net capture, particularly due to mission feasibility? Responds to "Assessments" in that their method is unreliable.

Quotes:

(Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods, p.204)
The majority of the methods have a lack of experimental verification phases, which is very essential in the growth of the concept.

(Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods, p.204)
Based on the technological readiness and maturity level within the sample, an analysis was conducted to ascertain which method can reach the production phase with current knowledge (Fig. 10). The readiness level was calculated by assigning points based on three criteria (model, simulation and experiment) for all the publications in each method. Then, the aggregate score based on the sampled count on a particular method was determined. The results showed that tether-based method and dynamical systems-based method have excelled in their capacity.

(Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods, p.204)
It has been determined that all the ADR systems are at a conceptual/experimentation phase and require more study to be established into commercially viable platforms. Different ventures have already capitalized on the emerging trends to start standalone ADR programs. Based on the focus of research and observations, the tether-based method and dynamical systems-based method are at the forefront. Further on, the concept of a mixture of methods is also catching on.

(Review of Active Space Debris Removal Methods, p.198)
The Monte Carlo analysis to analyze performance of methods is not sufficient to determining facts. Any conclusion has to be verified experimentally to confirm.
